3	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Olive Oil
Unique Mechanism
EXAMPLE
Olive Oil of the Month Club
(00:02)
All right. So the first unique mechanism example we’re going to look at is for
olive oil and this is a letter that Gary Bencivenga wrote for Mike Geary and it’s
about basically an olive oil of the month club. This is a great letter. At first,
I had heard about it actually a couple of years back and then Justin Goff,
who’s my partner in Copy Accelerator, has used it as a teaching example for a
variety of reasons and it’s really an awesome letter that I love so much. Why
do I love it? Well, it’s just really hard to make olive oil seem super sexy and yet
Gary found a way to do it. So you’re going to have a link to the whole letter and
you’ll be able to look through this and check it out and it’s really great.
(00:54)
If you look at just the headline, “Announcing a special offer for friends to
Mike Geary from the Olive Oil Hunter. If you’ll pay just $1 to help cover my
shipping costs, I’ll send you a complimentary $39 bottle of one of the greatest
olive oils I’ve ever discovered. Rushed to your table directly from the new
harvest at the peak of flavor and nutritional goodness, yours by exclusive
arrangement with one of the world’s top gold-medal-winning artisinal farms.
Available nowhere else in America. Independently lab-certified to be 100%
extra virgin olive oil. This means that you’ll never again have to worry about
unwittingly consuming, or feeding your family, Mafia-made olive oil. Yours as
a risk-free introduction to my Fresh-Pressed Olive Oil Club. No obligation or
commitment to buy anything - now or ever. RSVP today so you don’t miss this
special $39-bottle-for-a-buck offer. We have just 240 bottles of this sublime
olive oil to give away for just $1 to readers of Mike Geary. If you’re reading
this message, it means we still have some. But if you’d like one, please don’t
delay. They are going fast and it’s first come, first serve.”
(01:52)
So that kind of sets up the offer for you in the headline and the stuff here and
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 11 --

4	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Olive Oil
we do have an interesting thing about this old Olive Oil Hunter guy, the feather
in his cap which is always a classic and I’ll give you a little bit of a lead, too.
“Dear friend of Mike Geary, a major olive oil opportunity is at hand. But if you’re
interested, you must act quickly, If you’ll pay just $1 to help cover my shipping
costs, I’ll send you, with my compliments, a $39 bottle of rare, fresh-pressed
olive oil superior to any other oil you’ve ever tasted. It’s normally impossible
to find here in America.
(02:29)
The freshness, flavor, and health benefits of this just-pressed all oil will astonish
you. It will utterly blow away any olive oil you can find in the US and that’s for
one simple reason: this is fresh-pressed, supreme extra virgin olive oil—raced
here by jet straight from the olive oil harvest and it’s independently certified to
be 100% pure extra virgin. But what’s so special about “fresh-pressed” olive oil
direct from harvest and why can’t you find it in US supermarkets or gourmet
stores? Regardless of where you shop, do you know the most important secret
of how to choose the best-tasting extra virgin olive oil? It’s very simple. Olive
oil, unlike wine, does not improve with time. Just the opposite. It’s at its peak
of nutritional perfection and flavor immediately after it’s pressed. Olives, after
all, are a fruit and olive oil is a fruit juice. Just as with any other fruit juice, it’s
most delicious when fresh-pressed and that’s when you want to enjoy it.
(03:21)
But virtually none of the fresh-pressed olive oil from Italy—or any other great
olive-growing country—ever makes it to our shores. The reason is because
shipping by air is prohibitively expensive. As a result, virtually all the olive oil
sent to the US travel here by molasses-slow cargo ship. Adding insult to injury,
many oils are shipped months—maybe even a year—after pressing. Moreover,
olive oil is not shipped as a perishable food. To save money, producers send it
in container ships along with scores of manufactured goods—cars, machinery,
whatever—and the oils are subject to brutal conditions and worst of all, delays.
(03:57)
There are flavor-sapping delays at every port of call on this long ocean voyage,
while sitting at anchor in the harbor waiting for customs inspections and FDA
approval before admits. Then there are further delays at the docks, in trucking
transport and in warehouses before eventual distribution to local stores. Then
these oils may sit yet additional weeks, months, or even years on shelves under
fluorescent lights, which also destroy flavor and precious, perishable nutrients.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 11 --

5	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Olive Oil
(04:21)
And now you know why the producers of ordinary olive oils seldom if ever print
a “harvest” date, sometimes also called the “pressing” date on their labels.
Sure, they will print a “best used by” date, but not the harvest date. I suspect
they want to extend shelf life as long as possible. They realize that savvy
olive oil lovers prize freshness above all else and they don’t want to give you
any hint as to how old and stale their oils may be after their torturously slow
journey from overseas to your shopping cart.”
(04:48)
There is a piece of proof here, “I can prove this to you right now. If you have a
bottle of olive oil in your kitchen pantry, please check to see if it has a harvest
or pressing date on the label in addition to the “best used by” date. If you
bought your oil in a supermarket or gourmet store, I can almost guarantee that
it does not have a harvest date. And since there’s almost never a harvest date
on the label of ordinary olive oils, so many of us foodies can be seduced by
fancy bottles and pretty labels only to bring home mediocre olive oils whose
flavors have faded and dulled—and sometimes have even turned rancid.”
(05:19)
I read a good amount of copy, but one of the reasons why is because what I
just read after the headline is really the mechanism of the problem because
again, how do you differentiate your olive oil from other people’s olive oil?
You’re selling olive oil, but you could argue olive oil is a commodity. It’s on the
shelves of every supermarket, maybe there’s boutique olive oil. How do you
really kind of make your olive oil stand out, that’s the big question.
(05:48)
So like I said, if you look at the mechanism behind the problem, it’s what I just
read, that “olive oil, unlike wine, does not improve with time. Just the opposite.
It’s at its peak of nutritional perfection and flavor immediately after it’s pressed.
Olives, after all, are a fruit and olive oil is a fruit juice. Just as with any other
fruit juice, it’s most delicious when fresh-pressed and that’s when you want to
enjoy it.” I think that alone, I love that whole component of olives being a fruit
and just like with fruit juice, it’s most delicious when fresh-pressed.
(06:21)
That’s a great piece of what we call testable proof; something that you can do
in your head, think about and be like, “Oh, that makes sense.” The other piece
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 11 --

6	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Olive Oil
of testable proof that we saw, like I mentioned briefly is the looking at olive
oil in your pantry and how it doesn’t have a pressed by date or whatever. Has
a used by date, but not the date that it was pressed and that’s more testable
proof that backs up or supports the mechanism. That’s what testable proof is.
(06:49)
So as you look through this, this is what the mechanism behind the problem
is. What about the mechanism behind the solution? Well, I pulled this from
the sales letter and I could have it up, but I’ll just show you guys what it is and
again, you can look at the sales letter to see. But if you go through here, you’ll
see there’s more copy and stuff like that and this discovery story and things
we’ll talk about later. But as far as the unique mechanism behind the solution
goes, here it is.
(07:17)
“Long story short, that’s how I founded the Fresh-Pressed Olive Oil Club. I
realized that if I were willing to break with the tradition of shipping olive oil
by slow cargo ship and instead rush it to America by jet, direct from the new
harvest of each olive-growing country, I could be the first to bring authentic
fresh-pressed oil to foodies here in America all year round.
(07:36)
Four times a year— roughly in November, February, May and August, I travel the
world, stalking the global olive groves to sample hundreds of fresh-pressed
olive oils right at the peak of their harvest-fresh flavor. My trusted sources
in these faraway olive groves—established over my years of globetrotting—
include hundreds of the finest boutique producers and traditional family-
owned estates whose oils are proud local treasures. These oils are normally
produced in small, exclusive batches and are reserved for high-end restaurants
and a gourmet clientele in their own locales. Many are the top award winners
in regional olive oil competitions. They are virtually never shipped to the US.
(08:12)
When I arrive at these harvests, the most respected artisanal producers roll
out the welcome mat for me. They know I represent club members who are the
most discriminating olive oil aficionados in America and who fully appreciate
fresh and exquisitely flavorful oils. Indeed, our club’s membership consists
of many chefs, high-end restaurants and hotels, as well as a wide array of
gourmets, foodies and health-conscious individuals from every walk of life
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 11 --

7	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Olive Oil
who insist on enjoying—and serving their lucky guests—the most flavorful and
healthiest olive oils on earth.
(08:42)
Our club’s demanding reputation precedes me and I usually find myself being
treated like a visiting raja. The region’s top artisanal producers vie with one
another to bring me dozens of their finest fresh-pressed oils to make me smile
with pleasure.” Such a good copy. “From these, I choose the three brightest,
most flavorful oils. Then I race my newfound treasures at the peak of their
harvest-flavor and nutritional goodness to the US by jet plane and then right
to your front door by rush delivery. The result is that, as a club member, you
get to enjoy what only a tiny handful of Americans ever get to experience-true,
fresh-pressed olive oil and all its amazing taste and health benefits, rushed to
you straight from the latest harvest.”
(09:24)
That’s the mechanism of the solution. So do you guys understand the
connection? The problem is basically that most olive oil takes months or years
to get to you, it’s shipped in brutal conditions, it’s not fresh - I want to make
sure I had a call that interrupted my headphones for a second, I got to turn
my phone on to flight mode. So it’s not fresh and I think that’s the mechanism
behind the problem so if that’s the problem, the solution is to basically break
with tradition and rush it to America by jet direct from the new harvest of each
olive-growing country and again, this is a packaged consumer good and it
makes so much sense. You totally buy the solution.
(10:20)
Now you have to have the problem of being a gourmet or a food lover or
somebody who’s a lover of olive oil. But if you do and you read about the
problem, then the solution just makes perfect sense to you. You’re like, “Man,
I want that, too. I want the freshest oil,” and I get it. If they’re shipping it, first
of all, the quality. He’s being more discerning, but then he’s shipping by jet, it
gets to you faster so it’s really fresh, peak harvest, all of these things. That’s
the mechanism of the solution and then one other thing I’ll say, and I’m going
to do some other examples as well, but the one thing I’m going to point out
here is that you want to...what’s my point I’m trying to make of this? Lost my
train of thought for a second, shit. Well, if I remember that point, I will put it
in the next video and I probably will remember that point. But I think this is a
good explanation so let’s look at some more examples.
